text.content:
Welcome, first-time DM. This module is built so you can focus on running a great Dark Souls night instead of juggling rules. Here is everything you actually need to do.

The 60-second pitch to your players

"You are Unkindled — restless dead who refuse to stay down. You will die tonight. A lot. That is the point. Death sends you back to the last bonfire a little wiser; the world never changes, but you do. Read your enemies, learn their tells, and you will win. This is Dark Souls at the table."

What to prep before session one

  1. Read the Soulslike Rules journal once (you can skim the cheat sheet). Players only strictly need the Defensive Reaction System page.
  2. Read Run-of-Show (next page) so you know the spine of the night.
  3. Read Running the Defensive System — this is the one new thing you operate at the table.
  4. Skim the four boss Moveset Cards and their Tell Ladders (in the Bestiary / DM Codex).
  5. Have players pick pregens (Pregen Characters compendium). No character building required.

The only three things you operate

  • The Souls pool — one shared number on the Party sheet. Award it when they kill things; spend it when they level/shop. Macros do the math.
  • The bonfire loop — when they rest: full heal, respawn enemies, offer leveling. That's it.
  • The windowed defense — when an enemy attacks, narrate three beats and let the target react. (Full how-to two pages on.)

Golden DM rules for this game

  • Never say "you die." The world is deadly; let their choices spring the trap. (See Teaching Deaths.)
  • Telegraph everything. Big danger always gets a visible wind-up. Fair beats surprising.
  • Reward reads, not stats. When a player correctly calls a tell, lean in and let it land hard.
  • Let them win when they earn it. If a clever party trivializes a fight by reading it perfectly — that is a triumph, not a problem.

The night is four acts, roughly 4–6 hours. Times are guides — compress by trimming hollows, expand by leaning into farming and the dragon.

Act 1 — Cemetery of Ash (60–75 min)

  1. Awakening. The PCs claw out of their graves as Unkindled. Cold open — no exposition dump. They start at level 3.
  2. Tutorial hollows. Crawling hollows, then a sword hollow. Teach the four base actions (Strike, Dodge, Block, Parry), Estus, and that death is not the end. Let someone drop and recover so the death rules land.
  3. Cemetery bonfire. First rest. Show them the loop: heal, refill Estus, respawn enemies.
  4. IUDEX GUNDYR — first major boss, 2 phases. He is the forgiving teacher: clean, readable moveset. Reward → level 4–5.

Act 2 — Firelink Shrine (hub, 30–45 min)

A breather and spend-souls hub. All four NPCs are available:

  • Fire Keeper — level up (spend banked souls).
  • Shrine Handmaid — shop (Estus shards, catalysts, consumables).
  • Andre — upgrade weapons and the Pyromancy Flame with titanite.
  • Sword Master — optional duel + the summon-sign mercy valve for later.

Deliver the lore + plot hook, then warp to the High Wall.

Act 3 — High Wall of Lothric (90–120 min)

  1. High Wall bonfire (entry).
  2. Hollow Courtyard. Hollow soldiers; this is the designated soul-farm pocket (respawns on rest) for struggling tables. → L5–6.
  3. The Dragon. Optional secret boss on the rampart — three resolution paths (bypass / drive off / hunt to kill). See its card.
  4. OUTRIDER KNIGHT — mini-boss 1, a fast brutal duel that punishes turtling. → L6–7.
  5. Shortcut unlock. A lift/ladder loops back to the bonfire.
  6. PUS OF MAN — mini-boss 2; a "dead" hollow erupts into an abomination. Fire-weak. → L7–8.
  7. Pre-Vordt bonfire — right outside the fog gate. Zero run-back.

Act 4 — Climax (45–60 min)

  1. VORDT OF THE BOREAL VALLEY — final boss, 2 phases. Frostbite build-up, wild charges, frost-brittle joints weak to fire/thunder. At 50% HP he frenzies (the exam). → L8–10.
  2. Epilogue. The frost lifts; the gate to the Undead Settlement opens. Sequel hook, fade out.

If you are running short on time

  • Skip the Dragon (it is explicitly optional).
  • Trim the Hollow Courtyard to one wave; only farm if the party is underleveled.
  • Cut Pus of Man and route straight to the pre-Vordt bonfire.
  • Run Gundyr and Vordt at the lower HP targets.

If you have extra time

  • Run the full Dragon hunt (path C) for a real secret-boss payoff.
  • Let them farm the courtyard and over-level — Vordt is more fun when they feel strong and STILL have to read him.
  • Lean into Firelink roleplay with the NPCs.

This is the one new system you operate live. It is simple once you have run two attacks. Here is the DM-side procedure.

On an enemy's turn, for each attack

  1. Pick the move (boss) or use the enemy's single move (mook). Open its Moveset Card.
  2. Narrate the three windows out loud, in order, with a beat between them: W1 wind-up, W2 commit, W3 impact. Make the telegraph vivid — that is the fairness.
  3. Ask the targeted player: "How do you answer — Dodge, Block, or Parry, and on which window?"
  4. Look up that cell on the card's Defensive Profile grid → it tells you ADV / normal / DIS / impossible.
  5. They roll one flat d20 (no ability mod) at that quality vs the move's DC. Resolve.

How to narrate windows (examples)

"Vordt hauls the great mace up over his shoulder, frost steaming off it (W1)… he steps in and the head comes screaming down in an overhead arc (W2)… it's about to crater the stone where you stand (W3). What do you do?"

Players declare against whichever window they think is the opening. The grid does the rest.

Resolving each defense

Dodge (free)Flat d20 vs Dodge DC. Success: no damage, move them 5 ft; if behind the enemy, their next attack has advantage. Failure: full damage.
Block (costs reaction)No roll, deterministic: subtract the shield's set % per damage type (its card). Chips at low tiers; never staggers. Charges / grabs / shield-kicks are unblockable — they show — on the Block row.
Parry (costs reaction)Flat d20 vs Parry DC; only the ideal window is ADV, all else DIS. Success: negate + stagger the enemy (it loses its next action) and the whole party gets a riposte (free attack, treat as advantage/crit-friendly). Failure: full damage.

Keeping it fast

  • Only the target reacts. For an AoE move, each creature in the area reacts (Dodge only, usually).
  • A declaration is one phrase plus one d20. Don't let it become a negotiation.
  • Mooks have ONE move — after the first exchange the table already knows the answer, so trivial fights fly by.
  • Don't reveal the grid. Reveal it through play and through Tell Ladders on wipes.

Impossibility tags

Each move lists what cannot beat it: unblockable (grabs, magic), undodgeable (tracking charges — block or eat it), unparryable (anything not a clean weapon swing). Enforce these — they are what make each move a distinct puzzle.

Calibrating difficulty on the fly

  • Too easy? Use the boss's nastier moves and its legendary actions; tighten DCs by 1–2.
  • Too hard / table frustrated? Volunteer a Tell Ladder rung early, soften a DC, or have the Sword Master summon sign appear.

When the party rests at a bonfire

  1. Everyone to full HP; refill all Estus and spell charges; clear Exhaustion and build-up.
  2. Respawn all non-boss enemies in the area (reset their tokens). Bosses stay dead.
  3. At the Firelink bonfire only: offer leveling via the Fire Keeper (spend banked souls → apply a Level-Up Card). Other NPCs are available here too.

Awarding souls

Use the Award Souls macro (adds to the Party pool) whenever they kill something. Targets:

Common hollow30–60
Elite100–150
Mini-boss (Outrider, Pus)1,000
Gundyr2,500
Vordt3,000
Dragon (kill, path C)3,000–4,000 + unique drop

Level costs (spent at Firelink)

500 / 800 / 1,200 / 1,800 / 2,600 / 3,600 / 5,000 souls for levels 4 → 10. Boss kills alone cover roughly four levels; farming and the dragon cover the rest.

Death — two cases

One PC hits 0 HPNormal 5e death saves. Allies can stabilize/heal. The fight continues. Nothing is lost.
Whole party down (wipe)Respawn everyone at the last bonfire. Reset the boss and area enemies. Run the Drop Bloodstain macro: their carried souls become a bloodstain token at the death spot, and you advance the boss's Tell Ladder by one rung.

The bloodstain

Place a bloodstain token where they fell. If they reach it, run Reclaim Bloodstain (returns the souls). If they wipe again first, delete the old bloodstain (gone forever) and drop a new one. Don't soften this — the threat of loss is the engine of caution.

The mercy valve

After several wipes on the same wall, drop the Sword Master summon sign near the fog gate. If used, place his controllable phantom token to fight alongside them (DS3 co-op). He fades when the boss dies or the party rests.

pages.name: The Four Teaching Deaths (Run Them Fair)

text.content:
Lethality is designed, never scripted. You never declare a death; the world is genuinely dangerous and the party's own choices spring the trap. In hindsight, every death was avoidable.

The five rules of a fair Souls death

Before any near-certain death lands, all five should be true:

  1. Telegraphed — they could see it coming (a cue, scorched corpses, the boss rears back to inhale).
  2. Self-inflicted — their choice sprang it (charged the bridge, got greedy, clustered up).
  3. Soft consequence — death is respawn + dropped souls, not a dead character.
  4. Legible lesson — it points at exactly one fixable mistake.
  5. Exploitable in hindsight — the thing that killed them is the key to winning.

The four moments (each teaches a transferable skill)

1. Gundyr Phase 2The party "wins," relaxes, low on Estus — then the Pus of Man erupts from his corpse and mauls them. Lesson: victory isn't victory until the bar's empty; save resources.
2. The DragonThey charge the open rampart and a full-bridge fire breath torches the cluster. Lesson: positioning over DPS; use cover, get behind it.
3. Outrider KnightA shield-camping, passive party gets ground down by relentless pressure (he hastes if not attacked). Lesson: aggression and footwork beat turtling.
4. Vordt Phase 2 (the exam)At 50% HP he frenzies: a wild charge through the cluster plus frostbite stacks → Exhaustion → cascade. Lesson: spread out, manage the status bar, bait the charge.

How to spring one without forcing it

  • Lay the telegraph clearly, then let them choose. If they read it and play safe, reward that — no death required.
  • If they take the bait, let the consequence be real and total. Then, at the bonfire, make sure the lesson is legible (a Tell Ladder rung, an NPC line, an obvious environmental clue).
  • Never repeat the same teaching death twice. Once they've learned it, that danger becomes a tool they can exploit.

How Tell Ladders work

Each boss has a prepared Tell Ladder: a sequence of escalating hints about its exploitable openings (the Defensive Profile, its weakness, its rhythm). The party climbs the ladder by dying. Each wipe, hand them the next rung — always delivered in-fiction, never as a stat sheet.

  • Rung 1 is a nudge ("the corpses are all clustered mid-bridge").
  • Middle rungs name a concrete opening ("his overhead leaves him wide open right as it lands").
  • Final rung hands them the kill ("beneath it, the fire can't reach and the belly is soft").

Crucially, the Ladder gives knowledge, not power — no stat buffs, no power creep. The party gets stronger by understanding. Full ladders for each boss live on their cards in the Bestiary / DM Codex.

Two ways past every wall

If a party is stuck, remind them (in-fiction) they always have both options:

  • Get smarter — climb the Tell Ladder, read the moveset.
  • Get stronger — retreat, farm souls at the courtyard pocket, level at the Fire Keeper, return.

Scaling for party size (3–6)

3 playersDrop boss HP to the low end of its range; reduce legendary actions by one; thin mook waves. Push the Sword Master summon earlier.
4 playersRun as written (the baseline).
5–6 playersUse the high HP end; add 1 legendary action; add an extra hollow or two per wave; bosses may target two PCs per round with separate moves.

Adjusting on the fly

  • Watch Estus economy — if the party is consistently dry before bosses, add an Estus Shard to the courtyard loot or soften a wave.
  • If a fight is a cakewalk, that's fine for mooks; for a boss, escalate with its nastier moves rather than just inflating HP.
  • Keep the night moving toward Vordt — that climax is the payoff. Don't let early farming sprawl past your time budget.
